central_question: How did one handwritten prison petition force the US Supreme Court to guarantee appointed counsel for all felony defendants — and what system did that ruling actually create?

subquestions.question: What exactly did Gideon v. Wainwright hold?
On what constitutional basis — which Amendment(s) and which doctrine?
What was Clarence Earl Gideon charged with and what happened at his original 1961 trial?
Who was his appointed attorney on retrial (Abe Fortas) and what was the result?
What prior precedent did Gideon overturn (Betts v. Brady, 1942) and why?
Was the decision unanimous, and who wrote the opinion (Hugo Black)?
What system did the ruling create — public defender offices, funding, caseload pressure?
How does the right to counsel connect forward to Miranda (right in practice vs. right in warning)?

primary_sources.description: Gideon v. Wainwright, 372 U.S. 335 (1963) — full opinion
Oyez case summary — oral argument audio and summary
Betts v. Brady, 316 U.S. 455 (1942) — overruled precedent

queries: Gideon dissent (none — unanimous, but Harlan concurrence nuance)
public defender office history United States
right to counsel indigent defendants funding

source_requirements: Each critical claim cites 372 U.S. 335 (primary opinion).
Betts v. Brady holding confirmed from primary opinion text.
Gideon's original trial facts confirmed from opinion or NARA record.
At least one institutional source (Oyez / LOC) corroborates the holding.
stop_conditions: Every critical subquestion answered from 372 U.S. 335.
No critical claim relies on a single non-independent secondary source.
Betts v. Brady holding confirmed for accurate 'overruled' claim.
